content:
# Hebrews Chapter 5 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.1 — "taken from among men" vs. "is a human being chosen"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "every high priest taken from among men is appointed on behalf of men" | ex anthrōpōn lambanomenos... hyper anthrōpōn kathistatai |
| LOGOS | "every high priest chosen from among men is appointed to act on behalf of men" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Every high priest is a human being chosen to represent other people" | "Represent" for kathistatai; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. kathistatai means "is appointed/constituted" — a formal designation, not merely "chosen to represent." The high priest does not merely represent people; he is constituted by God to act on their behalf. PNEUMA's restructuring also loses the parallel between "from among men" and "on behalf of men" — the high priest comes from humanity and serves humanity.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.5 — "today I have begotten you" vs. "Today I have become your Father"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "You are my Son, today I have begotten you" | sēmeron gegennēka se (Ps 2:7) |
| LOGOS | "You are my Son, today I have begotten you" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "You are my Son. Today I have become your Father" | Reverses direction (same as HEB 1:5) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Same issue as 1:5 — PNEUMA reverses the begetting direction. The Psalm 2:7 quotation is about the Son's generation, not the Father's becoming.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Recurring PNEUMA pattern — reversal of Ps 2:7.

---

### v.9 — "having been made complete/perfect" vs. "God qualified him as a perfect High Priest"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having been made complete, he became the cause of eternal salvation" | teleiōtheis egeneto... aitios sōtērias aiōniou |
| LOGOS | "being made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "God qualified him as a perfect High Priest, and he became the source of eternal salvation" | Adds "God," "qualified," "High Priest"; restructures |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says Christ was "made perfect" (teleioō — brought to completion through suffering) and became the "cause/source" (aitios) of eternal salvation. PNEUMA adds "God qualified him as a perfect High Priest" — inserting both the agent (God) and the office (High Priest) where the author leaves them implicit. The subtlety of the passive ("being made perfect") allows multiple agents; PNEUMA specifies one.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA over-specifies the passive.

---

### v.12 — "the basic principles of the oracles of God" vs. "the basic things about God's word"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the elementary principles of the oracles of God" | ta stoicheia tēs archēs tōn logiōn tou theou |
| LOGOS | "the basic principles of the oracles of God" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the basic things about God's word" | "Things" for stoicheia; "God's word" for logiōn tou theou |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. stoicheia means "elements/rudiments/basic principles" — a technical term. logia means "oracles" — divine utterances, not merely "word." PNEUMA reduces both technical terms: stoicheia to "things" and logia to "word." The author uses elevated language deliberately — these are the foundational elements of God's oracular pronouncements.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA reduces technical terms.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA reverses Ps 2:7 | 1 | "I have become your Father" for "I have begotten you" (v.5) — recurring pattern |
| PNEUMA over-specifies passives | 1 | Adds agent and office to teleioō (v.9) |
| PNEUMA reduces technical terms | 1 | stoicheia → "things," logia → "word" (v.12) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 5's disagreements are moderate. The recurring Ps 2:7 reversal (v.5, also 1:5) is the most notable — PNEUMA consistently renders "I have begotten you" as "I have become your Father," reversing the direction across multiple citations. The v.9 over-specification is also significant: the author's use of the passive ("being made perfect") is theologically deliberate, and adding "God qualified him as a perfect High Priest" collapses the passive's openness.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
